agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes reducing ETA granularity from exact timestamps ('Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows ('Delivery by Friday'). In logistics, ETA precision is a core differentiator and directly tied to customer satisfaction, SLA enforcement, and operational planning. This is not a cosmetic UI change — it redefines the delivery promise the business makes to every customer.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Customer-Facing ETA","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — the delivery promise communicated to the recipient, which may differ from internal operational estimates."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"The time range within which a delivery is promised. Can be exact (timestamp), hourly, or date-level."},{"term":"SLA (Service Level Agreement)","definition":"Contractual or implied commitment to deliver within a stated timeframe. Violations may trigger penalties or refunds."},{"term":"Last Mile","definition":"The final leg of delivery from distribution hub to recipient. Most variable and hardest to predict accurately."},{"term":"Delivery Promise","definition":"The customer-facing commitment that sets expectations. Breaking it erodes trust regardless of operational reasons."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A delivery ETA must always be communicated to the customer after order confirmation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without an ETA, the customer has no delivery expectation and cannot plan — this is a fundamental logistics contract.","testable_assertion":"Every confirmed order has a non-null ETA visible to the customer within the tracking interface."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"ETA precision level must be consistent across all customer-facing channels (email, SMS, tracking page, app).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Showing 'Friday' in the app but '14:30 Friday' in email creates conflicting promises and erodes trust.","testable_assertion":"Given an order, the ETA displayed on every channel uses the same granularity and value."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"If ETA granularity changes, historical orders must retain the ETA format that was originally promised.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Retroactively changing a precise promise to a vague one after the customer relied on it violates the delivery contract.","testable_assertion":"Orders created before the cutover date display their original timestamp-level ETA, not the new date-only format."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"SLA compliance must be measured against the granularity of the promise made — not retroactively reinterpreted.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the customer was promised '14:30 Friday' and delivery arrives at 18:00 Friday, that is an SLA miss even if it arrived 'on Friday'.","testable_assertion":"SLA breach detection uses the ETA granularity that was active when the promise was made."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Date-only ETA implies end-of-business-day as the delivery deadline for operational purposes.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a policy decision — 'by Friday' could mean by 17:00, 20:00, or 23:59. A VP can define this.","testable_assertion":"When ETA is date-only, internal systems treat [configurable cutoff time] on that date as the SLA deadline."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A new order is confirmed after the feature is enabled","when":"The system calculates and displays the ETA","then":"The ETA is shown as a date-only window (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday') on all customer-facing channels"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order was confirmed before the feature was enabled with an exact timestamp ETA","when":"The customer views the tracking page","then":"The original exact timestamp ETA is displayed, not the new date-only format"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A date-only ETA of 'Friday' is promised","when":"The delivery is completed at any time on Friday before the configured cutoff","then":"The delivery is marked as on-time and no SLA breach is recorded"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A date-only ETA of 'Friday' is promised","when":"The delivery is completed after the configured cutoff on Friday or later","then":"The delivery is marked as an SLA breach"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"The ETA format changes to date-only","when":"Downstream systems (notifications, analytics, partner APIs) consume the ETA","then":"All systems handle the reduced-precision format without errors or misinterpretation"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A premium/express service tier exists","when":"An express order is placed","then":"The system evaluates whether date-only precision is appropriate for that tier or if timestamp precision must be retained"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed late Thursday for Friday delivery — 'Delivery by Friday' gives almost no useful information to the customer.","expected_behavior":"System should evaluate whether the remaining window is too short for date-only to be meaningful and potentially show a more precise estimate."},{"scenario":"Cross-timezone deliveries where 'Friday' is ambiguous — sender's Friday vs recipient's Friday.","expected_behavior":"Date-only ETA must be interpreted in the recipient's local timezone, clearly labeled."},{"scenario":"Partner/B2B integrations that contractually require timestamp-level ETAs.","expected_behavior":"API contracts must continue to serve timestamp precision where required; date-only may be customer-facing only."},{"scenario":"Customer calls support asking 'what time on Friday?' — support agents need internal timestamp estimates even if customers see date-only.","expected_behavior":"Internal operational ETAs retain timestamp precision; only customer-facing display changes to date-only."},{"scenario":"Multi-package orders where items arrive on different dates — 'Delivery by Friday' per-item vs per-order.","expected_behavior":"Each package should have its own date-level ETA; order-level ETA reflects the latest package."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Reduced ETA precision lowers the bar for SLA compliance (more deliveries become 'on-time' under date-only measurement), which could reduce refund/penalty payouts. However, if competitors offer precise ETAs, this could drive customer attrition — particularly in premium segments where delivery certainty commands a price premium. Potential revenue risk from reduced competitiveness.","operational":"Operationally, date-only ETAs reduce pressure on last-mile scheduling precision, potentially lowering dispatch optimization costs. However, it also removes a key feedback signal — if ETAs were frequently wrong at the timestamp level, the root cause (poor route prediction, hub delays) is now masked rather than fixed. Support call volume may increase as customers call to ask 'what time today?'","user":"Significant negative impact on customer experience. Customers plan their day around delivery windows — 'by 14:30' lets them step out in the morning; 'by Friday' forces them to stay available all day. This is especially painful for B2C deliveries requiring signature or for high-value goods. Customer trust and satisfaction will likely decline.","risk":"High risk of masking operational problems rather than solving them. If the motivation is that exact ETAs are often wrong, switching to date-only hides the inaccuracy instead of improving prediction. Risk of contractual breach with B2B partners who require timestamp precision. Risk of competitive disadvantage as industry trends toward more precise, not less precise, delivery windows.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Customers (Recipients)","impact":"Lose ability to plan their day around delivery. Forced to remain available for longer periods. Likely increase in missed deliveries and redelivery attempts."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Expected increase in inbound inquiries asking for more precise delivery times, increasing support load and cost."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Reduced pressure for timestamp-level accuracy may improve short-term efficiency but masks prediction model deficiencies."},{"group":"B2B / Enterprise Partners","impact":"May violate contractual SLA requirements that specify timestamp-level precision. Requires contract review."},{"group":"Product / Competitive Strategy","impact":"Moves against industry trend of increasing precision (Amazon, DoorDash, FedEx all trending toward narrower windows). Potential competitive disadvantage."},{"group":"Finance / SLA Compliance","impact":"Artificial improvement in on-time metrics due to wider compliance window. Distorts operational KPIs."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The proposal lacks critical context: WHY is this change being made? Is it because exact ETAs are frequently inaccurate? Are there cost pressures? What customer segments exist and do any have contractual timestamp requirements? Without understanding the motivation, I cannot assess whether this is a pragmatic improvement (admitting imprecision honestly) or a regression (hiding a fixable problem). The spec also does not address service tiers, B2B contracts, or transition strategy.","high_confidence_areas":["The domain rules around ETA consistency and historical promise preservation are well-established logistics invariants","Customer impact of reduced precision is well-understood in the industry","Edge cases around timezone, support workflows, and partner APIs are standard logistics concerns"],"low_confidence_areas":["Motivation for the change — is current timestamp accuracy poor?","Existence and terms of B2B/enterprise SLA contracts requiring timestamp precision","Whether service tiers (standard vs express vs same-day) exist and how they should be handled","Current ETA accuracy metrics that would justify or contradict this change","Regulatory requirements around delivery time communication in operating jurisdictions"],"assumptions":["The system currently has both customer-facing and internal operational ETAs","Multiple customer-facing channels exist (app, email, SMS, web tracking)","Some form of SLA measurement exists today based on timestamp ETAs","The change is intended for all service tiers unless specified otherwise"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"The proposal does not state WHY exact timestamp ETAs are being replaced with date-only windows. Is it because timestamps are frequently inaccurate? Cost reduction? Simplification? The motivation fundamentally changes whether this is a good or bad business decision. If timestamps are wrong 60% of the time, date-only may be more honest. If they're right 95% of the time, this is a regression.","affected_stakeholders":["End Customers","Product Strategy","Operations"],"options":["Provide current ETA accuracy metrics to justify the change","Clarify if this is a temporary measure while prediction models improve","Identify if specific service tiers or regions are driving the request"],"recommendation":"Before approving this change, require the engineering team to provide current timestamp-level ETA accuracy data. If accuracy is below an acceptable threshold (e.g., <80% on-time within 30 minutes of stated ETA), date-only may be justified as an honesty improvement. If accuracy is high, this change should be rejected."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information provided about existing service tiers (standard, express, same-day) or B2B contractual obligations. Express and same-day delivery services fundamentally require sub-day precision — telling a same-day customer 'Delivery by today' is meaningless.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Partners","Premium Customers","Legal/Contracts"],"options":["Confirm whether express/same-day tiers exist and whether they are in scope","Review B2B contracts for timestamp precision requirements","Implement tier-based ETA granularity rather than blanket date-only"],"recommendation":"Exclude express, same-day, and any contractually-bound B2B deliveries from this change. Date-only may be acceptable for standard ground shipping only."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No transition or rollback strategy described. Changing the delivery promise format affects active orders, notification templates, partner API schemas, analytics dashboards, and SLA measurement. A cutover plan is essential.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Operations","Customer Support","Analytics"],"options":["Define a cutover date with grandfathering for in-flight orders","A/B test date-only vs timestamp with a customer subset first","Implement as a gradual rollout with monitoring"],"recommendation":"Require an A/B test or phased rollout with clear success metrics (customer satisfaction scores, support call volume, missed delivery rate) before full deployment."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes reducing ETA granularity from exact timestamps ('Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows ('Delivery by Friday'). In logistics, ETA precision is a core differentiator and directly tied to customer satisfaction, SLA enforcement, and operational planning. This is not a cosmetic UI change — it redefines the delivery promise the business makes to every customer.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Customer-Facing ETA

content.thinking:
Analyzing the business impact of changing delivery ETAs from exact timestamps to date-only windows.

This is a domain analysis task about ETA display changes in a logistics/courier context. Let me think through this carefully.

Key considerations:
- Moving from "Delivery by 14:30 on Friday" to "Delivery by Friday" is a significant reduction in precision
- This affects customer expectations, operational planning, and potentially SLA enforcement
- I need to think about why the team might want this (maybe because exact times are unreliable?) but that's not stated

Missing context: Why is this change being proposed? Is it because current exact ETAs are frequently wrong? What's the accuracy rate? What SLAs exist? What customer segments are affected?


I'm recognizing this as a missing context problem — the proposal lacks critical details about motivation, current ETA accuracy, contractual SLA obligations, and which customer segments would be impacted. I have enough domain knowledge to work through the business rules and acceptance criteria, but I should flag what information gaps need to be addressed before moving forward.
